proposals.hypothesis: By formulating an integrated design optimization problem that includes a controllability metric derived from closed-loop MPC performance as an objective or constraint, it is possible to identify column designs that offer superior dynamic performance with minimal economic penalty compared to steady-state optimal designs.
proposals.method: The method involves a nested optimization architecture. An outer loop optimizes column design parameters using a steady-state economic objective. An inner loop evaluates dynamic controllability by solving a finite-horizon optimal control problem (the MPC core) for a set of representative disturbances, using a linearized dynamic model derived from the current design. Controllability will be quantified by a combined measure of the closed-loop output variance and constraint violation frequency. The approach will leverage Skogestad's work on self-optimizing control and steady-state degrees of freedom to structure the problem, using gradient-based optimization or surrogate models to manage computational cost.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a systematic, optimization-based framework for designing distillation columns for inherent controllability under MPC. It will yield quantitative guidelines on how specific design parameters influence dynamic performance, moving beyond heuristic rules. The result is a methodology to co-design the plant and its advanced control system for improved operational resilience.
proposals.novelty: While design for controllability exists, explicitly optimizing physical design parameters for the performance of a specific, constrained advanced controller like MPC is novel. It inverts the typical paradigm of applying MPC to a fixed design, instead letting the controller's capabilities guide the design process itself.

proposals.title: A Hierarchical, Multi-Timescale MPC Architecture for Safe Column State Transitions Inspired by Biological Homeostasis
proposals.motivation: Startup, shutdown, and load-change transitions in chemical process columns (e.g., distillation, reactive) are economically significant and operationally hazardous, often leading to off-spec product, equipment stress, and safety violations. Current industrial practice often relies on heuristic sequences or single-layer MPC not designed for the multi-timescale dynamics of such transitions. A systematic, optimal control framework for transitions is a critical unsolved problem in process operations.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical MPC architecture, explicitly separating fast (neural-like) and slow (hormonal-like) control loops and coordinated by a high-level path planner, can mimic biological homeostasis to achieve safer, more efficient, and more optimal transitions between complex operating states than conventional single-layer MPC or sequential procedures.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-layer framework: 1) A high-level 'master' MPC uses a reduced dynamic model to compute the optimal sequence of intermediate steady states (operating points) towards the target, respecting path constraints on thermal stress and safety. 2) This master MPC coordinates two lower-level MPCs: a 'fast' MPC for manipulated variables like flows and pressures, and a 'slow' MPC for variables like composition and catalyst activity, each with tailored sampling times and prediction horizons. The method will be tested via simulation on a high-fidelity dynamic model of a distillation column with pH considerations, extending Skogestad's prior aquaculture system modeling work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control architecture that extends MPC's utility from steady-state regulation to optimal dynamic transitions. It will deliver a generalizable design procedure for multi-timescale hierarchical MPC and demonstrate quantified improvements in transition time, energy use, and off-spec product reduction for a case-study column.
proposals.novelty: The explicit bio-inspired analogy to homeostasis for structuring hierarchical control in process transitions is novel. While multi-layer MPC exists, the principled decomposition into fast/slow loops directly mimicking autonomic nervous system coordination for smooth state transitions in chemical processes is a new application of this biological principle.

proposals.title: Dynamic Bus Arbitration as a Constrained Feedback Control Problem: A Self-Optimizing Control Approach
proposals.motivation: In modern multi-core and many-core systems, contention for shared resources like memory buses creates a critical performance bottleneck. Traditional static or heuristic arbitration schemes (e.g., round-robin, fixed priority) cannot adapt to dynamic, heterogeneous workloads, leading to suboptimal system throughput and latency. Framing this as a formal control problem, pre-2026, offers a principled way to manage this shared-resource constraint dynamically.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bus arbitration can be formulated as a constrained optimal control problem where the objective is to maximize overall system utility (e.g., weighted throughput), and that a feedback control law based on the principles of self-optimizing control can dynamically enforce the optimal arbitration policy under varying workloads.
proposals.method: We will model the bus as a constrained dynamic system where the inputs are the granted access times to requesting agents and the outputs are performance metrics (e.g., queue lengths, service rates). The core method will apply the self-optimizing control (SOC) framework, where we identify a set of controlled variables (CVs), such as weighted wait times, that when kept at constant setpoints, indirectly lead to near-optimal economic performance. We will develop a feedback controller, potentially using model predictive control (MPC) to handle constraints explicitly, that adjusts arbitration grants based on real-time measurements of these CVs. The optimization layer will periodically update the CV setpoints based on a steady-state economic objective (e.g., maximizing a defined system welfare function).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, control-theoretic methodology for designing dynamic bus arbiters with provable performance guarantees under constraints. It will deliver a concrete control architecture and tuning guidelines for system-on-chip (SoC) designers, moving beyond ad-hoc heuristics. The results will demonstrate how concepts from process control (SOC, MPC) can be effectively translated to solve core problems in computer systems engineering.
proposals.novelty: While control theory has been applied to network congestion and CPU scheduling, its application to low-level hardware bus arbitration using the self-optimizing control paradigm—which separates steady-state optimization from dynamic regulation—is novel. This approach systematically handles hard constraints and dynamic workload changes in a way that purely optimization-based or machine-learning-based schedulers pre-2026 do not.

proposals.title: Online Constraint-Aware Adaptive Tuning via Steady-State Back-Off Optimization
proposals.motivation: Many industrial processes, such as water treatment and recirculating aquaculture systems, operate under tight constraints and experience slow, unmeasured disturbances or parameter drift. Traditional feedback controllers with fixed tuning can become suboptimal or violate constraints as the process model changes, leading to economic loss or safety issues. An adaptive system that automatically re-tunes the controller to maintain optimal, constraint-satisfying operation is needed for robust and economical performance.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a steady-state real-time optimization (RTO) layer that continuously identifies the active constraints and optimal operating point with a model-based tuning rule generator, an adaptive control system can maintain near-optimal regulatory performance despite process changes.
proposals.method: The method builds on the 'plantwide control' procedure of identifying active constraints and self-optimizing variables. A supervisory layer will use steady-state data reconciliation and gradient estimation (pre-2026 methods) to update the identified process model and active constraint set. This information will feed into an online calculation of appropriate linear controller tuning rules (e.g., SIMC rules) for the selected control structure. The adaptive loop will be event-triggered by significant shifts in the estimated steady-state gain or dominant time constant.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for adaptive control that explicitly links steady-state economic optimization with dynamic controller tuning. It will deliver a practical algorithm for processes where model identification for full MPC is too costly, extending the applicability of self-optimizing control concepts to adaptive systems. The result is a scalable method for maintaining optimal operation under drift and disturbance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, online coupling of constraint-seeking RTO with simple, rule-based PID tuning updates, creating an adaptive scheme that is both economically driven and simple to implement. This moves beyond standard model reference adaptive control by using economic optimality, not just tracking performance, as the criterion for re-tuning.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Optimization for Robust IMC-PID Tuning with Explicit Constraint Handling
proposals.motivation: Internal Model Control (IMC)-PID tuning is a cornerstone of industrial process control, prized for its simplicity and single tuning parameter (λ). However, the standard rule-of-thumb for selecting λ to balance performance and robustness is often inadequate for complex, constrained systems like water networks or recirculating aquaculture systems. A systematic, optimization-based tuning framework is needed to handle multiple, often conflicting, objectives such as setpoint tracking, disturbance rejection, and input usage, while explicitly respecting actuator and state constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: Formulating IMC-PID tuning as a constrained numerical optimization problem will yield controller parameters that systematically outperform classical IMC rules, providing a superior Pareto-optimal trade-off between performance and robustness for a wider class of process models, including those with significant time delays and inverse responses.
proposals.method: The method will formulate a multi-objective optimization problem where decision variables are the PID parameters (or the IMC filter time constant λ). The cost function will combine weighted norms of tracking error, control effort, and a robustness metric like the peak of the sensitivity function (Ms). Constraints will include limits on manipulated variable rates and magnitudes. We will employ gradient-based optimization (e.g., sequential quadratic programming) and potentially multi-objective genetic algorithms to map the Pareto front. The approach will be tested on benchmark process models (e.g., FOPTD, SOPTD) and applied to case studies inspired by Skogestad's prior work, such as the pH control loop in the recirculating aquaculture system model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, automated, and flexible framework for PID tuning that bridges the gap between classical IMC simplicity and modern optimal control. It will deliver a set of tuning rules or a software tool for generating PID parameters that are Pareto-optimal with respect to user-defined performance and robustness weights, explicitly accounting for system constraints.
proposals.novelty: While optimization for PID tuning exists, the novelty lies in embedding the well-understood IMC structure and its robustness guarantees directly into the optimization problem's constraints and objectives, creating a hybrid method that retains IMC's intuitive foundation while optimizing its key parameter(s) beyond the single λ rule.

proposals.title: Robust Constraint-Handling Control Design using Convex Approximations of Nonlinear Model Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Robust control theory, including Skogestad's work on self-optimizing control and constraint handling, predominantly relies on linear time-invariant (LTI) uncertainty descriptions. This is a significant limitation for process systems with strong nonlinearities (e.g., pH in water treatment, recirculating aquaculture) or time-varying parameters, where LTI uncertainty leads to overly conservative or invalid guarantees. Extending robust design to broader uncertainty classes is essential for practical, high-performance control.
proposals.hypothesis: By describing process uncertainty using convex, time-varying sets derived from a nonlinear nominal model (e.g., via differential inclusions or linear fractional transformations with nonlinear blocks), one can synthesize less conservative controllers that explicitly handle constraints and maintain near-optimal operation over a wider operating region.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage the concept of transformed inputs for linearization to obtain an affine-in-inputs nominal model. Nonlinear/time-varying uncertainty will be embedded as a convex set in the parameters of this model. We will then formulate a robust optimal control problem, similar to Model Predictive Control (MPC), where constraints are enforced for all realizations within this set. The synthesis will use iterative convex optimization (e.g., linear matrix inequalities or robust optimization techniques) to design a feedback law that minimizes a worst-case cost while ensuring constraint satisfaction, drawing on methods from integrated design and control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic control design framework for nonlinear processes with guaranteed robustness beyond the LTI paradigm. It will yield a practical methodology for handling hard constraints under realistic uncertainty, directly applicable to Skogestad's themes of water and energy systems. The result bridges the gap between nonlinear MPC robustness analysis and tractable robust controller synthesis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit combination of convex uncertainty descriptions for nonlinear dynamics with constraint-handling optimal feedback design, moving beyond the standard additive/multiplicative LTI uncertainty models prevalent in pre-2026 robust control synthesis.

proposals.title: Fundamental Performance Limitations for Constrained Systems with Structured Uncertainty and Mixed Input-Output Constraints
proposals.motivation: Classical performance limitation theory, such as the Bode integral and the waterbed effect, provides profound insights for linear, unconstrained systems. However, modern control applications, from energy management to process systems, inherently involve operational constraints (e.g., peak power, actuator saturation) and complex, structured uncertainty descriptions (e.g., parametric, real). Pre-2026 theory lacks a unified framework to quantify the fundamental trade-offs in such practically relevant settings, limiting the ability to predict achievable performance a priori.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating tools from convex optimization and dissipativity theory, it is possible to derive new, computable bounds on achievable performance (e.g., on integral error measures) for systems subject to mixed input-output constraints and structured, non-dynamic uncertainty, generalizing the classical linear, unconstrained results.
proposals.method: The approach will extend the framework of Skogestad and Postlethwaite (2005) and recent work on input-output constraints. We will formulate the performance limitation problem as a constrained optimization over the set of stabilizing controllers, using Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs) and Integral Quadratic Constraints (IQCs) to encode structured uncertainty. Specific algorithms will involve solving semi-definite programs to compute worst-case performance bounds, validated via case studies on constrained systems like the tiered power charge problem and recirculating aquaculture systems with pH constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic methodology for control engineers to assess the fundamental limits of performance in complex, constrained systems before controller design. It will yield new, explicit limitation theorems that subsume classical results as special cases, offering crucial insight for the optimal design and operation of advanced industrial systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit combination of structured uncertainty descriptions (beyond norm-bounded) with hard, mixed input-output constraints within a rigorous performance limitation analysis, moving beyond the linear, unconstrained, and full-block uncertainty assumptions of the classical theory.

proposals.title: Robust-Oriented System Identification: An Inverse Problem Approach for Constrained Process Control
proposals.motivation: Traditional system identification aims for high-fidelity models, but these models often lead to overly complex or fragile robust controllers. For critical constrained systems like water networks or recirculating aquaculture, a model optimized for nominal fit can perform poorly under uncertainty or when active constraints shift. There is a need to invert the design process: identify models that are explicitly tailored for subsequent robust control synthesis, ensuring the controller's performance and constraint satisfaction are prioritized.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating system identification as an inverse problem where the objective is to minimize the worst-case performance of a subsequently synthesized robust controller, the resulting models will yield simpler, more reliable controllers with guaranteed constraint handling compared to models from prediction-error methods.
proposals.method: The approach integrates constrained optimization and robust control theory. We will develop an iterative algorithm that alternates between: (1) synthesizing a structured H-infinity or mu-synthesis controller for a current model estimate, and (2) updating the model parameters to minimize the worst-case closed-loop performance (e.g., weighted sensitivity) subject to constraints on model complexity and fit to experimental data. The framework will explicitly incorporate input and output constraints from the process, using methods akin to reference governor or model predictive control concepts to guide the identification towards constraint-aware models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic methodology for control-oriented identification, directly linking model uncertainty descriptions to robust controller synthesis. It will deliver practical identification algorithms and software tools that produce models guaranteeing better closed-loop robust performance and constraint satisfaction for applications in process control and energy management.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is treating the robust controller synthesis step as an integral, differentiable part of the identification loss function, effectively inverting the conventional sequential design process. This moves beyond merely quantifying uncertainty for a given model to actively shaping the model and its uncertainty description for control.

proposals.title: Worst-Case Disturbance Synthesis for Robust Control Validation: A Constrained Optimization Approach
proposals.motivation: Robust control design, including Skogestad's work on constraint handling and feedback, aims to maintain performance under uncertainty. However, standard validation methods often rely on ad-hoc or historical disturbance profiles, which may not reveal a system's true vulnerabilities. A systematic method to synthesize the most challenging disturbances for a given control architecture is needed to rigorously stress-test designs, particularly for critical systems like water networks or energy management where constraint violations are costly.
proposals.hypothesis: The worst-case disturbance for a given robust control system, subject to realistic amplitude and frequency constraints, can be systematically synthesized by solving a dual optimization problem where the disturbance is the decision variable and the objective is to maximize a control-relevant cost function (e.g., constraint violation or integrated error).
proposals.method: We will formulate the disturbance synthesis as a dynamic optimization problem. The system model (e.g., a recirculating aquaculture or building energy model) and its implemented control law (e.g., MPC or decentralized PID with feedforward) are treated as fixed constraints. The disturbance sequence is the manipulated variable, bounded within a pre-defined feasible set (magnitude, rate, spectral content). We will employ direct transcription or sequential methods (e.g., single-shooting with gradient-based optimization) to solve this max-min problem, leveraging tools like CasADi and IPOPT. The approach will be tested on case studies from Skogestad's recent work on tiered power charges and aquaculture systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, optimization-based methodology for generating worst-case disturbance scenarios, moving beyond Monte Carlo or historical data for control validation. It will yield a practical tool for identifying critical weaknesses in control architectures and for guiding the redesign of controllers or the tightening of operational constraints to improve robustness.
proposals.novelty: While worst-case analysis is common in robust control theory (e.g., H-infinity), the active synthesis of constrained, physically realizable disturbances that maximize a practical control objective for a *given* (potentially sub-optimal) implemented controller is a distinct, application-oriented dual problem. It inverts the typical control design paradigm to focus on stress-testing.

proposals.title: Control-Relevant Nonlinear Model Reduction via Empirical Balanced Truncation and Optimal Input Design
proposals.motivation: Model reduction for nonlinear systems, such as those in water treatment and aquaculture, often focuses on open-loop fidelity, neglecting closed-loop control objectives. This can lead to poor controller performance or instability when the reduced model is used for design. A control-aware reduction framework is needed to ensure the preserved dynamics are those most critical for feedback, especially under operational constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: A model reduction procedure that explicitly incorporates the optimal control problem's cost function and constraints into the reduction criterion will yield reduced-order models that better preserve closed-loop performance compared to standard open-loop methods.
proposals.method: The approach will extend empirical balanced truncation for nonlinear systems by defining controllability and observability Gramians based on simulations driven by optimally designed inputs, not random perturbations. These inputs will be generated by solving a steady-state optimization problem (e.g., similar to Tiered Peak Power Charge management) that reflects the system's operational constraints and economic objectives. The resulting state trajectories will be used to compute a control-relevant empirical balancing transformation, followed by truncation. The method will be tested on benchmark nonlinear systems and a case study of a recirculating aquaculture system with pH dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, optimization-based methodology for generating reduced nonlinear models tailored for control design. It will deliver concrete guidelines for input design to inform reduction and demonstrate improved closed-loop performance in simulation for constrained systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of steady-state operational optimization (reflecting Skogestad's 'plantwide control' philosophy) directly into the model reduction process, creating a direct link between economic constraints, the reduced model's dynamics, and the ultimate control objective.

proposals.title: Dynamic Self-Optimizing Control: Subset Selection for Processes with No Steady-State Operation
proposals.motivation: The concept of self-optimizing control (SOC) and the selection of controlled variable (CV) subsets via the gradient method (gSOC) are powerful for steady-state operation. However, many modern processes, such as batch, cyclic, or highly dynamic systems like recirculating aquaculture or demand-response energy management, never operate at a true steady state. Applying steady-state SOC methods to these systems is fundamentally mismatched, limiting their economic performance and operational robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic gSOC subset selection method, formulated using time-averaged or integrated gradients of the operational economics over a representative dynamic cycle, can identify CV subsets that enforce near-optimal operation for processes that never reach a steady state.
proposals.method: The method will extend the steady-state gradient matrix, H, to a dynamic equivalent, H_dyn(t), evaluated along a nominal optimal dynamic trajectory. We will formulate a time-integrated loss function, L_dyn = avg(||H_dyn(t)^T (y(t) - y_opt(t))||^2), over the operational cycle. Subset selection will be performed by solving a mixed-integer optimization problem to minimize L_dyn, subject to structural constraints for implementability. The approach will be tested on a dynamic model of a recirculating aquaculture system (with pH and biomass dynamics) and a home energy management system under time-varying power prices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for selecting controlled variables for economic optimal operation of inherently dynamic processes. It will bridge the gap between steady-state SOC theory and the practical needs of batch, cyclic, and transient systems, enabling near-optimal feedback control without repeated online dynamic optimization.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the re-definition of the self-optimizing control problem and the associated selection metric for a purely dynamic operational paradigm, moving beyond the steady-state assumption foundational to all prior gSOC work.

proposals.title: Online Reconfiguration of Controlled Variable Sets for Multi-Mode Plant Operation
proposals.motivation: Modern chemical and water treatment plants increasingly operate in flexible, multi-mode configurations to handle varying feedstocks, product grades, or energy prices. Pre-2026 work on self-optimizing control (SOC) provides methods for selecting optimal controlled variables (CVs) for a given steady-state, but these selections are typically fixed offline. This is inadequate for plants that reconfigure online, leading to lost economic performance or constraint violations when the pre-selected CVs are no longer optimal.
proposals.hypothesis: An online algorithm can efficiently re-select a near-optimal subset of measurements as controlled variables in response to changing active constraints and economic conditions, maintaining near-optimal operation with guaranteed stability during transitions.
proposals.method: The approach builds on the null-space method for SOC and the concept of active constraint regions. We will develop a real-time algorithm that: 1) Monitors the gradient of the economic objective and constraint violations to detect region changes. 2) Uses a pre-computed library of optimal measurement subsets for each region, generated offline via structured H-infinity or loss-based selection. 3) Employs a switching logic based on hysteresis and a stability certificate for the reconfigured feedback system, inspired by pre-2026 work on switched systems and transformed inputs for decoupling. The online computation will be limited to checking simple conditions and switching between pre-validated controllers.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the practical applicability of self-optimizing control from fixed to reconfigurable plants. It will provide a systematic framework for real-time CV selection, ensuring sustained near-optimal operation under varying modes. The result is a direct contribution to the control of flexible, sustainable processes like recirculating aquaculture or tiered-energy systems.
proposals.novelty: While SOC selection is well-studied offline, the online, real-time re-selection of CV subsets in response to operational mode changes, with integrated stability-aware switching logic, is a novel and non-trivial extension of pre-2026 methods.

proposals.title: Optimal Operation of Distillation Columns Under Deliberate Vapor-Liquid Imbalance via Constraint-Handling Advanced Control
proposals.motivation: In distillation, conventional operation strives to maintain a balanced vapor-liquid (V/L) profile, often assumed to be the optimal steady state. However, this paradigm may enforce unnecessary operational constraints, limiting flexibility and energy-saving potential, especially under variable feed conditions or energy pricing. This work challenges the necessity of restoring this 'optimal' condition, proposing that operating under a controlled V/L imbalance could be more economically optimal if stability is ensured via feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating the traditional V/L balance as a soft constraint rather than a hard requirement, a distillation column can be operated at a dynamically controlled, imbalanced steady state that minimizes a defined economic cost function (e.g., energy use) while maintaining product specifications through advanced control.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer control architecture. The supervisory layer uses steady-state real-time optimization (SS-RTO) with an economic objective, explicitly relaxing the V/L balance constraint. The regulatory layer employs Model Predictive Control (MPC) with transformed inputs for linearization and decoupling, as per Skogestad's recent work, to stabilize the imbalanced column. The MPC will handle active constraints (e.g., product purity) and reject disturbances, ensuring stable operation at the RTO-set, potentially imbalanced, operating point. The system will be tested on a high-fidelity dynamic model of a binary distillation column.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a systematic framework for challenging deeply ingrained operational paradigms in process control. It will demonstrate how constraint relaxation coupled with advanced regulatory control can unlock new, more economical operating regimes. The results will contribute to the theory of optimal operation under constraints and offer practical insights for energy-intensive separation processes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately designing and stabilizing a distillation column at a steady state that violates the classical V/L balance rule, a fundamental heuristic in column design and operation. This moves beyond optimizing within conventional constraints to redefining the constraint set itself via control.

proposals.title: Dynamic Controllability and Operability Analysis of Petlyuk Dividing-Wall Columns with Vapor Recompression Heat Pumps
proposals.motivation: The Petlyuk dividing-wall column (DWC) offers significant energy savings for multi-component separations, but its complex dynamics and high degree of integration pose major control challenges. Integrating vapor recompression (VRC) cycles for heat pumping further enhances energy efficiency but introduces additional dynamic interactions and constraints, making the operability of the combined system a critical, unresolved question in process intensification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamic controllability of a Petlyuk DWC with integrated VRC is fundamentally limited by the propagation of thermal disturbances and the active constraints imposed by the compressor, and that a systematic analysis will reveal optimal control structures that prioritize constraint handling over setpoint tracking.
proposals.method: We will develop a rigorous, first-principles dynamic model of the integrated system. Using the concept of self-optimizing control, we will screen candidate controlled variables (CVs) to find linear combinations of measurements that minimize steady-state loss. Dynamic controllability will be assessed via frequency-domain analysis (e.g., Relative Gain Array, condition number) and dynamic simulations of key disturbances. The study will compare decentralized control structures with advanced model predictive control (MPC) formulations that explicitly handle the compressor's operational constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for assessing the dynamic operability limits of intensified distillation systems with heat integration. It will yield concrete guidelines for selecting robust control structures and identifying the trade-off between energy efficiency and dynamic performance in such systems.
proposals.novelty: While controllability studies exist for standalone DWCs, the explicit integration of VRC dynamics and its associated constraints into a rigorous dynamic operability analysis for the Petlyuk configuration is novel and addresses a key bottleneck in realizing its practical, industrial implementation.

proposals.title: Economic Model Predictive Control for Integrated Distillation Column and Compression Systems with Dynamic Constraint Handling
proposals.motivation: Integrated column and compression systems, common in gas processing and petrochemicals, face significant operational challenges due to tight energy coupling and variable feed conditions. Pre-2026, advanced process control (APC) applications in such systems often treat the compressor as a separate unit or use simplified models, missing opportunities for holistic economic optimization and dynamic constraint management of the coupled system.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a tailored Economic Model Predictive Control (EMPC) framework, which explicitly models the dynamic interactions and shared constraints between the column and compressor, will yield superior economic performance and smoother operation compared to decentralized or traditional regulatory control strategies.
proposals.method: The approach will develop a dynamic, first-principles model of the integrated system, including key constraints (e.g., compressor surge, column flooding, product purity). We will formulate a multi-input, multi-output EMPC problem using a linearized state-space model for prediction, solved via quadratic programming (QP) at each sampling interval. The controller will manipulate inputs like reflux rate and compressor speed to minimize a dynamic economic objective (e.g., operating cost) while respecting all process constraints. Implementation will follow the 'plantwide control' methodology, ensuring proper pairing of remaining regulatory loops.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete EMPC design and application case study for a critically important but challenging integrated process. It will demonstrate how to systematically handle the dynamic constraints of coupled unit operations to achieve direct economic benefits, contributing to the broader adoption of APC in energy-intensive industries.
proposals.novelty: While MPC is well-established, its application as a unified EMPC for the specific, tightly coupled column-compressor system, with explicit dynamic management of shared constraints like compressor surge during column upsets, is not thoroughly documented in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: From Robustness Margins to Tuning Parameters: Systematic PI/PID Tuning Rules Derived from μ-Analysis
proposals.motivation: Structured singular value (μ) analysis is a powerful tool for robust stability analysis but is rarely used directly for controller synthesis, especially for simple PID controllers widely deployed in process industries. Practitioners lack a clear, systematic bridge between the rigorous but abstract robustness margins from μ-analysis and practical, tunable controller parameters. This gap limits the adoption of advanced robustness analysis in everyday control design for systems like water networks or recirculating aquaculture, where operating constraints are critical.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicit, algebraic relationships exist between the achievable robustness margins from μ-analysis for common process dynamics and the optimal tuning parameters of PID controllers, enabling the derivation of tuning rules that guarantee pre-specified robust performance.
proposals.method: The method will first map common process model structures (e.g., first/second-order with delay) and uncertainty descriptions (e.g., input multiplicative) to their corresponding μ lower and upper bounds. Using constrained optimization, we will solve for the PID parameters (Kc, τI, τD) that maximize the robust stability margin (1/μ) for a given nominal performance specification, or vice versa. This will be repeated across a grid of normalized process parameters to generate contour maps, from which simplified, approximate tuning formulas will be regressed, akin to the Ziegler-Nichols method but with a robustness guarantee.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new set of practical PID tuning rules directly linked to quantitative robustness metrics, making μ-analysis accessible for routine control design. It will offer a principled alternative to traditional tuning methods by explicitly trading off nominal performance and robust stability, which is crucial for operating systems near constraints.
proposals.novelty: While μ-analysis is well-established, its direct inversion to produce simple, algebraic PID tuning rules for common process models is novel. The approach systematically embeds robust constraint satisfaction into the foundational step of controller tuning, moving beyond iterative analysis.

proposals.title: A Robust Operability Framework for Distillation Columns Integrating Essential Nonlinear Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Distillation columns, a cornerstone of chemical processes, exhibit significant nonlinear behaviors such as changes in relative gain and internal flow constraints that limit performance. Linear robust control frameworks, while powerful for handling uncertainty, often fail to guarantee performance across the full operating range because they neglect these essential nonlinearities. This gap restricts the economic operability of these energy-intensive systems, a critical pre-2026 concern for process intensification and sustainability.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically integrating low-order nonlinear models of key column behaviors (e.g., changes in separation factor with feed composition) into a structured robust control framework, the guaranteed region of operable performance can be significantly expanded beyond that predicted by purely linear robust analysis.
proposals.method: The approach will combine Skogestad's plant-wide control philosophy with structured singular value (μ) analysis. First, a nonlinear dynamic model of a representative distillation column will be used to identify critical nonlinearities affecting gain and dynamics. These will be captured via parameter-varying linear models or simple Hammerstein-Wiener blocks. The framework will then formulate the operability problem as a constrained optimization, where the controller must satisfy μ-based robust performance criteria across a defined set of operating points and nonlinear model parameters, effectively treating the nonlinear behaviors as structured uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, practically applicable methodology for designing controllers that are robust to both traditional model uncertainty and essential process nonlinearities. It will deliver a quantitative tool for defining the true operable window of a distillation column, directly linking control design to economic operation under varying feed conditions and product specifications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the structured integration of physically-derived nonlinear dynamic elements into a linear robust control synthesis and analysis framework, moving beyond simple linearization or gain-scheduling to provide rigorous, wide-range performance guarantees for a core unit operation.

proposals.title: Plant-Wide Control Integration for Distillation Columns: Managing Robust Local Constraints within a System-Wide Optimal Operation Framework
proposals.motivation: In chemical process plants, distillation columns are critical unit operations often controlled with robust, constraint-handling strategies like Model Predictive Control (MPC) to manage purity and flooding constraints. However, their performance is tightly coupled with upstream reactors and downstream separation units. Pre-2026 plant-wide optimization often uses a Real-Time Optimization (RTO) layer, but the interaction between local column constraints and the propagation of disturbances through the plant is not systematically analyzed, leading to suboptimal economic performance and potential constraint violations during transients.
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic, tiered control architecture that explicitly coordinates column-level robust constraint controllers with a plant-wide economic optimizer will yield superior dynamic economic performance compared to a decentralized approach, by ensuring that local constraints inform and are protected by the system-wide setpoint calculations.
proposals.method: The research will employ a hierarchical control structure. The top layer is a plant-wide economic Model Predictive Controller (EMPC) or a steady-state RTO coupled with a dynamic coordinator, formulated using gradient-based optimization to maximize a pre-2026 economic objective (e.g., net present value). The middle layer will consist of local MPC controllers for key columns, designed for robust disturbance rejection and constraint handling. The key innovation is the formulation of the coordination problem: the plant-wide EMPC will use transformed inputs and constraint mappings to generate setpoints that respect the active constraints of downstream/upstream units, while a dynamic feasibility test based on the back-off approach from Skogestad's work will ensure setpoints are achievable by local controllers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodological framework for integrating robust unit-level control with plant-wide economic optimization, specifically for processes dominated by distillation. It will deliver quantitative guidelines for designing the constraint communication and back-off calculations between layers. The result is a practical control design procedure that enhances overall plant profitability and resilience.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical control is known, the explicit treatment of robust local constraint regions as dynamic operability boundaries for the plant-wide economic optimization layer is novel. This moves beyond steady-state back-off analysis to a dynamic, constraint-aware coordination protocol, directly addressing the gap between unit-level robustness and system-wide optimality.

ideas.title: Study control of well-conditioned, high-gain processes
ideas.description: Analyze the dual problem: control design for well-conditioned but very high gain processes.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Structural Dynamics for Optimal Control Bandwidth
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'Control-Constrained Design' principle from aerospace engineering to process systems. In aircraft design, the airframe's structural dynamics are co-designed with control surfaces to ensure the vehicle's natural responses are controllable within the actuators' bandwidth. The analogous solution is to treat a chemical distillation or absorption column not just as a separation unit but as a dynamic structure with inherent modal responses (e.g., composition wave propagation, hydraulic lags). The adapted research idea formulates an inverse problem: optimize fundamental column design parameters (e.g., tray hydraulics, packing geometry, column aspect ratio) to shape the system's transfer function matrix—specifically its dominant time constants, zero locations, and gain structure. The objective function maximizes a metric of inherent MPC performance, such as the alignment of the system's slow dynamics with the MPC's prediction horizon or the minimization of non-minimum phase behavior that challenges constraint handling. This creates a 'control-aware' column design that is inherently easier and more robust to control with modern MPC systems, moving beyond steady-state economics to embed dynamic controllability into the physical scaling laws of the unit operation.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Fidelity Nonlinear MPC with Surrogate Model Correction
ideas.description: This approach implements a Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) strategy that dynamically switches between model fidelities to balance real-time computational feasibility with thermodynamic accuracy. A fast, computationally inexpensive surrogate model handles the online optimization, while an embedded high-fidelity nonlinear model runs asynchronously to monitor for significant prediction drift. When the high-fidelity model detects a deviation beyond a pre-set tolerance, it corrects the surrogate model's state or parameters, or directly adjusts the control sequence. This creates a robust, self-correcting controller that maintains the benefits of first-principles modeling without the crippling online computational cost.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective MPC Tuning via Pareto-Optimal Parameter Evolution
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel method for tuning Model Predictive Control (MPC) parameters by directly incorporating economic objectives (e.g., energy consumption, product yield) alongside traditional tracking performance. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary ecology and multi-objective optimization, where Pareto optimality defines trade-offs between competing life-history strategies, we adapt the solution of Pareto-front exploration. The adapted idea is to frame MPC tuning as a multi-objective optimization problem seeking the Pareto-optimal surface between competing control objectives (e.g., setpoint tracking vs. actuator energy, product purity vs. throughput). We propose developing an automated tuning algorithm, inspired by algorithms like NSGA-II, that systematically maps this trade-off surface. This provides operators with a clear visualization of achievable performance compromises, enabling informed selection of MPC parameters based on prevailing economic conditions and priorities, rather than relying on ad-hoc weighting of costs in the objective function.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Robust Control with Model Predictive Economic Layer Adaptation
ideas.description: This research idea proposes a hierarchical control architecture inspired by principles from Evolutionary Developmental Biology. The analogous problem in Evo-Devo is how biological systems maintain operational robustness while adapting to changing environments. This is solved through a separation of timescales: robust, conserved core processes handle immediate perturbations, while slower adaptive mechanisms tune the system for long-term fitness. Adapted to process control, the solution is to tightly integrate a low-level robust multivariable controller (e.g., H-infinity or Mu-synthesis based) that guarantees stability and performance for ill-conditioned plants under uncertainty, with a higher-level economic Model Predictive Optimization (EMPC) layer. The EMPC layer performs real-time economic optimization on a slower timescale or with move suppression, dynamically providing optimal setpoints or adjusting tuning parameters within the stability-preserving envelope of the robust controller. This creates a system that is both dynamically robust and economically adaptive.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Model Predictive Control with a Biological Coordination Mechanism
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea of scaling MPC for a distillation column to plant-wide control by drawing an analogy from systems biology, specifically the hierarchical coordination of the neuroendocrine system. The analogous solution is the multi-tier, feedback-regulated architecture of axes like the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal system, which coordinates a stress response across different organs and timescales. The adapted idea proposes a hierarchical MPC framework for chemical plants. A high-level 'plant coordinator' MPC acts as the master regulator, optimizing for global economic objectives and sending target trajectories to intermediate 'section coordinator' MPCs (e.g., for separation trains). These, in turn, send setpoints to the 'unit-level' MPC controllers (including the distillation column and connected units). Crucially, dynamic performance and constraint data from the unit level are fed back up the hierarchy as modulating signals, allowing the higher-level controllers to adjust their targets in a coordinated, constraint-respecting manner. This biologically-inspired architecture aims to solve the scaling problem by decomposing the plant-wide optimization into manageable, interacting sub-problems with built-in coordination feedback, analogous to hormonal regulation.

ideas.title: Adaptive Buffer Management for Multi-Variable Process Control
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that composition, pressure, and level must be controlled simultaneously to fixed setpoints. Drawing an analogy from TCP congestion control in computer networking, where data flow is managed by dynamically adjusting the sending rate within a congestion window to prevent buffer overflow while maximizing throughput, we propose a novel control strategy for chemical processes. The adapted solution treats pressure and level as dynamic safety buffers with soft, adaptive constraints, while focusing primary control effort on composition. The controller would dynamically calculate safe operating envelopes for pressure and level based on real-time process conditions, allowing these variables to float within these envelopes to absorb disturbances and provide degrees of freedom for optimal composition control. This approach prioritizes preventing catastrophic failures (hard constraint violations) while optimizing the primary performance metric (composition), similar to how TCP prioritizes avoiding buffer overflow while maximizing data throughput within the available network capacity.

ideas.title: Frequency-Domain Robust MPC Synthesis via Dynamic Integral Quadratic Constraints
ideas.description: This research develops a novel robust MPC synthesis framework that merges mu-synthesis tools with dynamic, frequency-domain uncertainty descriptions. The core innovation is to integrate the receding horizon optimization of MPC with the analysis machinery of Integral Quadratic Constraints (IQCs), which can capture complex dynamic uncertainties (e.g., actuator bandwidth, neglected flexible modes) as frequency-dependent operators. The synthesis problem is formulated to design an MPC policy that explicitly minimizes a worst-case cost bound over a horizon, where the worst-case is evaluated over all uncertainties satisfying the dynamic IQCs. This results in a computationally tractable, though challenging, convex optimization problem that yields controllers with less conservative performance guarantees than those based on static uncertainty bounds, while retaining the constraint-handling and preview capabilities of MPC.

ideas.title: Adaptive MPC for Micro-Distillation using Neuromorphic Control Principles
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of implementing fast-sampling MPC for small-scale columns with rapid dynamics by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and neuromorphic engineering. The analogous solution involves the use of spiking neural networks and event-based sensing for low-latency, energy-efficient robotic control, where control actions are triggered by significant sensory events rather than a constant clock. Adapted to the research context, this leads to the proposal of an event-triggered Model Predictive Control (MPC) strategy for micro-distillation or lab-scale columns. The controller would monitor key, fast-responding process variables (like temperature profiles) and execute a new MPC optimization cycle only when these variables deviate beyond a dynamically adjusted threshold, signaling a significant state change. This approach reduces the average computational burden, enables faster effective sampling rates when needed to handle transients, and better matches the controller's action to the actual pace of the process dynamics, improving performance and efficiency for small-scale, rapid systems.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Robust MPC with Learned Sensor Dynamics and Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework that robustly handles ill-conditioned plants with unknown dynamics and imperfect sensors simultaneously. Instead of separate physical models, it uses data-driven techniques to construct a joint uncertainty description that encompasses both the plant's behavior and the sensor's dynamics, including delays and noise characteristics. The controller's predictions and constraints are then robustified against this combined, learned uncertainty set. This enables reliable performance in applications where high-fidelity models are unavailable and sensor imperfections are significant but poorly characterized.

ideas.title: Benchmarking MIMO Controllers via a Multi-Criteria Tournament Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the multi-criteria tournament framework from comparative phylogenetics to the benchmarking of advanced MIMO controllers for distillation columns. In phylogenetics, competing evolutionary trees are evaluated against multiple, distinct optimality criteria (parsimony, likelihood, etc.) under data perturbations to identify the most robust hypothesis. Analogously, this work proposes to benchmark Model Predictive Control (MPC), decoupling control, and robust H-infinity control not on a single metric, but through a structured tournament across a suite of conflicting performance and robustness criteria (e.g., tracking error, control effort, stability margins, computational demand). The controllers will be tested across a designed spectrum of distillation column operating conditions and disturbance scenarios. The outcome will be a nuanced, multi-dimensional profile for each controller, identifying which strategy is most robust and suitable for specific operational priorities, rather than declaring a single universal 'best' controller.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Distributed MPC for Plant-Wide Control via Modular Agent Coordination
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, scalable framework for plant-wide control by replacing a single, monolithic MPC with a network of coordinated, modular MPC agents. Each major process unit is governed by its own fast, dedicated MPC agent with a high-fidelity local model. A supervisory economic MPC layer, using reduced-order models and plant-wide economic objectives, dynamically coordinates these agents by adjusting their setpoints and constraints. This architecture maintains computational feasibility, improves operational resilience by containing faults, and enables scalable integration of new units into the control network.

ideas.title: MPC for Column State Transitions Using Biological Homeostasis Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the principle of biological homeostasis—where organisms smoothly transition between complex states like sleep and wakefulness—to the problem of chemical column startup and shutdown using Model Predictive Control (MPC). In physiology, the autonomic nervous system hierarchically coordinates fast (neural) and slow (hormonal) control loops to maintain stability during transition. Analogously, we propose developing a dual-layer MPC sequence: a high-level 'master' MPC defines the optimal path through intermediate operating states, while coordinating lower-level 'fast' MPCs (for rapid variables like flow and pressure) and 'slow' MPCs (for gradual variables like composition and catalyst activity). This bio-inspired, multi-timescale architecture aims to safely and efficiently transition distillation or reaction columns between operating states, minimizing off-spec product, thermal stress, and unsafe conditions, thereby scaling the dual-use of MPC for both steady-state and transitional operations.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Frequency-Weighted Uncertainty Modeling with Adaptive Complexity Reduction
ideas.description: This approach creates a structured uncertainty description that captures frequency-dependent dynamics while remaining computationally tractable for control design. It employs a hierarchical framework that separates high-fidelity dynamic uncertainty models for analysis from simplified, over-approximated versions for synthesis. Adaptive model reduction techniques automatically adjust the complexity of the uncertainty description based on the control bandwidth and performance requirements. The resulting system enables controllers that explicitly account for frequency-varying uncertainty characteristics while maintaining computational feasibility for real-time implementation in complex systems.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Adaptive Robustness: Dynamically Trading Performance for Stability
ideas.description: This research develops a control architecture that dynamically adjusts its robustness guarantees based on real-time assessment of system ill-conditioning and operational context. Instead of a fixed, worst-case design, it uses an online supervisor to modulate the controller's conservatism, ensuring stability is only enforced with the necessary level of performance degradation. This allows the system to operate with high performance during nominal conditions and automatically invoke stronger, performance-sacrificing safeguards only when severe anomalies are detected. The result is a controller that is both highly usable and extremely robust, avoiding the impracticality of permanently degraded operation.

ideas.title: Adaptive MPC Tuning via Neuromodulatory-Inspired Reinforcement Learning
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational neuroscience—specifically, the role of neuromodulators in dynamically tuning neural circuit parameters—to the problem of auto-tuning Model Predictive Control (MPC). In the brain, neuromodulators like dopamine adjust learning rates and temporal windows in real-time based on context. Analogously, we propose using a reinforcement learning agent not for direct control, but to generate a modulatory signal that continuously and adaptively tunes an MPC controller's prediction horizon, control horizon, and cost weights. The RL agent learns from operational performance feedback (a reward based on tracking error, control effort, and constraint violations) to optimize these parameters online. This allows the MPC to automatically adapt its planning characteristics, such as shifting from a short, reactive horizon to a long, predictive horizon as process dynamics or objectives evolve, mimicking the brain's context-sensitive optimization of computational resources.

ideas.title: Safe RL-MPC Tuning via Digital Twin Exploration and Constraint-Aware Meta-Priors
ideas.description: This approach uses a high-fidelity digital twin of the physical plant as a safe sandbox for an RL agent to extensively explore and learn optimal MPC horizon and weight tuning policies without risking the real system. A meta-reinforcement learning stage pre-trains a prior policy on a diverse ensemble of system models to provide robust, cautious initialization. For online deployment, this prior is adapted using real operational data but is governed by a safety filter layer that projects RL suggestions into a verified safe parameter subspace. This ensures all online tuning adjustments respect strict stability and performance constraints, making the auto-tuning process both effective and provably safe.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control via Condition-Number Estimation, Inspired by Seismic Retrofitting
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from seismic retrofitting in structural engineering to the problem of managing varying condition numbers in control systems. In retrofitting, a structure's changing dynamic properties are monitored to adapt its damping systems. Analogously, we propose to develop an online estimation module that continuously computes or infers the plant's condition number (or a proxy like the minimum singular value of a relevant matrix) from operational data. This real-time diagnostic signal is then fed into a supervisory adaptation layer. This layer does not rely solely on a single MRAC law, but uses the condition number estimate to schedule controller gains, re-weight objectives in a multi-criteria optimization, or switch to a pre-computed robust controller from a bank, thereby maintaining closed-loop performance and numerical robustness as the plant's characteristics change with the operating point.

ideas.title: Fault-Tolerant MPC with Adaptive Sensor Model Validation and Graceful Degradation
ideas.description: This research develops a Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework that is robust not just to known sensor noise and delays, but to unmodeled sensor dynamics and sudden sensor faults. The core innovation is a co-design of the MPC optimizer with a real-time sensor health monitoring system that validates the assumed sensor model against incoming data. If a significant deviation is detected—indicating a fault, changed delay, or mischaracterized noise—the controller autonomously switches to a pre-computed, robust backup control strategy to maintain system safety. This ensures the controller's performance degrades gracefully under sensor model uncertainty, moving beyond simple incorporation of sensor dynamics to active management of sensor reliability.

ideas.title: Validate MPC Controller via a Staged, Instrumented Pilot Plant, Inspired by Aerospace Wind Tunnel Testing
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from aerospace engineering's use of wind tunnels to validate CFD models, this idea adapts the concept of staged, instrumented physical testing to process control. The analogous solution involves using scaled, heavily instrumented models in controlled environments to collect rich validation data. Adapted to the research context, the future work involves testing the tuned MPC controller on a physical pilot-scale distillation column that is equipped with a dense array of sensors to measure key variables at multiple points. The validation will be conducted as a structured experimental campaign, where the controller is put through a predefined matrix of set-point changes, feed disturbances, and operational constraints. This generates a high-fidelity data set to rigorously quantify discrepancies between simulated and real-world controller performance, ensuring robustness before scaling to full industrial implementation.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Hybrid Subspace Identification for Nonlinear Process Columns
ideas.description: This approach develops high-fidelity column models by merging data-driven system identification with embedded physical knowledge. A subspace identification method captures the dominant linear dynamic relationships from operational data, while concurrently, predefined nonlinear function blocks model critical steady-state phenomena like vapor-liquid equilibrium. A joint estimation algorithm identifies the parameters of both the linear dynamic subsystem and the static nonlinearities, ensuring the composite model respects both the temporal data and the underlying process physics. This results in a robust, interpretable model valid across a wider operating envelope than purely black-box or purely linear methods.

ideas.title: Data-Informed Physical Uncertainty Structure Identification for Robust Control
ideas.description: This research develops a systematic framework that first identifies the correct mathematical structure of physical uncertainty from experimental data before deriving a parametric model. The approach uses designed excitation signals and statistical validation tests to discriminate between potential uncertainty representations (e.g., nonlinear static vs. dynamic, bounded vs. stochastic). The output is a validated, structured uncertainty description paired with a quantified confidence measure, enabling the subsequent synthesis of robust controllers with guaranteed performance that are tailored to the true physical character of the system.

ideas.title: Event-Triggered Hybrid Estimation for Real-Time MPC: Combining Fast Disturbance Observers with Adaptive MHE
ideas.description: This system integrates a fast, low-complexity disturbance observer (DOB) running at the controller rate with a slower, adaptive Moving Horizon Estimator (MHE) to ensure real-time feasibility without sacrificing robustness. The DOB provides continuous state and disturbance estimates for the MPC, while the MHE is activated only when triggered by a significant innovation error, updating the internal model and observer gains. This hierarchical approach maintains the constraint-handling and disturbance-rejection benefits of MPC-MHE while making it implementable on hardware with limited computational resources. The adaptive MHE corrects for persistent model mismatch, ensuring long-term accuracy where the linear DOB may fail.

ideas.title: Biological Homeostasis-Inspired Control: Sacrificing Peak Efficiency for Guaranteed Stability in Ill-Conditioned Systems
ideas.description: This research explores control designs that prioritize guaranteed stability over strict performance in the face of severe ill-conditioning, drawing a direct analogy from systems physiology and homeostasis. In biological systems, stability is maintained through redundant, overlapping regulatory pathways with high safety margins—such as antagonistic hormone pairs and saturating responses—which accept sub-optimal metabolic efficiency to ensure survival across diverse and unpredictable conditions. Adapting this solution, we propose designing controllers that mimic homeostatic principles by incorporating conservative, saturating, and antagonistic control actions. This involves using high gain margins, slow and bounded integrators to prevent wind-up, and deliberately trading off fast, optimal transient response for the robust, guaranteed stability needed in severely ill-conditioned or poorly characterized systems, ensuring operation remains within a safe 'envelope' at the cost of peak performance.

ideas.title: Modeling Uncertainty with Frequency-Dependent Impedance, Inspired by Material Science
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that system uncertainties can be adequately described by static bounds or gains. Drawing an analogy from material science, where the static 'rule of mixtures' was superseded by frequency-dependent complex moduli to model composite materials under dynamic loads, we propose a parallel solution for control theory. The adapted research idea is to model actuator and process uncertainties with a 'dynamic uncertainty impedance'—a frequency-dependent transfer function description. This provides a richer, more physically accurate representation of how uncertainty manifests across different operational frequencies, enabling the synthesis of robust controllers that are less conservative and better tuned to real-world dynamic imperfections.

ideas.title: Adapting Immunological Memory Concepts for MPC Disturbance Rejection
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending MPC tuning methodologies to explicitly handle disturbance rejection scenarios, such as feed composition or flow rate upsets, moving beyond traditional focus on setpoint tracking. By drawing an analogy from immunology, where the adaptive immune system learns and remembers pathogens to mount faster future responses, we adapt this solution to process control. The adapted idea is to develop a novel MPC architecture with an integrated 'immunological memory' module. This module would catalog disturbance patterns and the corresponding effective control actions, enabling the controller to recognize recurring or similar disturbances and deploy a pre-optimized, tailored response strategy, thereby significantly improving dynamic performance and robustness in the face of process upsets.

ideas.title: Adapting Nonlinear Control from Seismic Isolation to High-Purity Distillation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from seismic isolation in structural engineering. In that field, the solution to protecting buildings from unpredictable, high-energy ground motions is not to make the structure infinitely stiff but to introduce a controlled, nonlinear interface (like lead-rubber bearings) that isolates and dissipates energy. The adapted research idea applies this philosophy to high-purity distillation, a process notoriously ill-conditioned with strong nonlinearities. The goal is to develop control strategies that incorporate designed nonlinear elements—such as variable-structure or hysteresis-based controllers—into the actuation or feedback loop. This 'nonlinear isolation layer' would be tuned to absorb the impact of large disturbances and operational shifts, thereby managing the process's ill-conditioned behavior directly in its nonlinear regime, rather than relying solely on linear approximations around a nominal operating point.

ideas.title: Assumption-Aware Robust Control Synthesis via Interactive Trade-Off Exploration and Contract Formalization
ideas.description: This research develops a synthesis framework that replaces opaque simplification with guided, transparent negotiation of robustness-performance trade-offs. The tool interactively helps the practitioner map practical uncertainty descriptions (e.g., parameter ranges, delay margins) to structured uncertainty models, then visually explores the Pareto-optimal frontier of achievable performance versus uncertainty size. The final output is not just a controller, but a formalized 'robustness contract'—a clear, verifiable set of assumptions and guaranteed properties—ensuring the practitioner understands and agrees to the limits of the design's robustness before deployment.

ideas.title: Adaptive Kalman Filtering for MPC with Delayed, Noisy Measurements
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the common MPC assumption of instantaneous, perfect state feedback by incorporating realistic sensor dynamics. Drawing an analogy from sensor fusion in Inertial Navigation Systems (INS) and Global Positioning Systems (GPS), where GPS measurements arrive with known latency and noise, we adapt the solution of delayed-measurement Kalman filtering. The adapted idea is to develop a modified MPC architecture with an integrated state estimator that explicitly accounts for a fixed measurement delay. The estimator will maintain a buffer of past state predictions. Upon receiving a noisy delayed measurement, it performs the Kalman update against the buffered state prediction from the time the measurement was actually taken, not the current time. This yields a statistically optimal corrected state estimate for that past time, which is then propagated forward through the system model to the present to serve as the initial condition for the MPC's optimization horizon. This approach systematically addresses both sensor noise and the temporal misalignment caused by delay, leading to more robust and realistic MPC performance.

ideas.title: Topological Control for Non-Ideal Distillation Networks
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of binary separation ideality by extending control strategies to complex distillation systems (multicomponent, azeotropic, reactive). Drawing an analogy from Network Science, where controlling complex systems with missing direct links is achieved through topological analysis of network structure, we adapt this solution. The adapted idea, 'Topological Control for Non-Ideal Distillation Networks,' proposes to model distillation systems as thermodynamic state networks where azeotropes and reaction equilibria define the connectivity. Control strategies would then be developed to identify and manipulate key topological features (e.g., using concepts akin to centrality or community detection) to find and enforce viable separation trajectories that bypass traditional barriers, fundamentally shifting the control paradigm from direct composition targeting to navigation within a constrained state-space graph.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune-Inspired Moving Horizon Estimation with Memory
ideas.description: The original idea is to combine Model Predictive Control (MPC) with Moving Horizon Estimation (MHE) to handle unmeasured disturbances and model mismatch. By drawing an analogy from computational immunology—specifically, the adaptive immune system's use of immunological memory for efficient pathogen response—we adapt its core solution. The analogous system maintains a memory of past antigens for rapid recall. Adapted to the research context, this leads to the idea of an Adaptive Immune-Inspired Moving Horizon Estimation with Memory. This MHE framework would not treat each estimation window as entirely independent. Instead, it would build and curate a memory bank of past estimated disturbance sequences and state trajectories. Upon encountering a new situation, a similarity-matching algorithm would recall the most relevant historical profile to warm-start the MHE optimization over the current moving horizon. This adaptation directly addresses the core challenge by leveraging historical estimation 'experience' to improve the speed and robustness of disturbance and state estimation, thereby providing a better initial condition for the accompanying MPC, especially for systems with recurring or slowly evolving operational regimes and model errors.

ideas.title: Adapting Probabilistic Seismic Hazard Analysis to Derive Structured Physical Uncertainty Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the core methodology of Probabilistic Seismic Hazard Analysis (PSHA) from seismology to the domain of robust control. In PSHA, diverse and uncertain geophysical data is systematically integrated into a unified probabilistic hazard model. The adapted idea proposes to treat physical uncertainties in actuators and components as analogous to seismic sources. A general procedure will be created to: 1) characterize each physical uncertainty source (aleatory variability and epistemic model uncertainty), 2) structure their interactions and propagation through the system using logic trees and equivalent 'attenuation functions' that map parameter uncertainties to performance deviations, and 3) synthesize a final structured uncertainty model (e.g., a set of probabilistic weighting functions or stochastic differential equations) that explicitly quantifies the combined effect for direct use in robust control design tools like H-infinity or stochastic optimal control.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Signal Processing for Subspace Plant Identification
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from geophysics to the problem of developing column models from plant data. In seismic exploration, algorithms like MUSIC and ESPRIT are used for blind source separation to identify distinct wavefront arrivals from overlapping sensor signals, characterizing subsurface layers without prior source knowledge. By analogical transfer, these subspace separation techniques can be adapted to industrial process data: treating plant sensor outputs as mixed signals from multiple underlying dynamic sub-processes (reaction kinetics, mass transfer, heat exchange). The adapted approach would automatically decompose column operational data into constituent dynamic modes and time delays, generating interpretable grey-box models that reveal the physical sub-process contributions, going beyond black-box state-space models from conventional subspace identification methods.
